coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent carbon dots for enantioselective sensing of L-lysine and pH discrimination in vivo and in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Dan
Zhao, Zhonghua
Shi, Hu
Feng, Jianyang
Yang, Yongxing
Shi, Lihong
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescent probes with wavelength-resolved dual target sensing capacity are highly desirable for the simultaneous recognition of target objects. A novel fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with a yield of 32% was simply synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of neutral red and urea, resulting in discrete dual-emission bands (440 nm/542 nm) under single-wavelength excitation of 380 nm. It was discovered that the fluorescence (FL) signal of CDs at 440 nm enhances with the addition of L-Lysine, whereas the FL intensity at 542 nm remains invariable. Surprisingly, no significant luminescence response is observed after the addition of D-Lysine, implying that as-prepared CDs can serve as fluorescent nanoprobes for chiral recognition of Lysine. On the contrary, it was discovered that the FL band at 542 nm is highly sensitive to pH in linear ranges of 2.0–3.4 and 3.4–8.0, while the FL band at 440 nm is inert to it, providing an effective tool for the ratiometric detection of pH in a “turn-on” mode. Confocal imaging experiments further verify that this nanoprobe can monitor L-Lysine and pH fluctuations in biological systems in real time using ratiometric methods. We believe that our research will provide new insight into the suitable construction of prompt and straightforward ratiometric fluorescent probes for the simultaneous differentiation of two analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescent
L-Lysine
Chiral recognition
pH
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of L-Lys and pH
Fluorescent imaging in vitro and in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption of CDs. (B) Excitation-emission spectra of CDs (λex 320 −420 nm).
(A) FL spectra of CDs upon adding different concentrations of L-Lys (0 −700 μM). (B) Linear relationship of Ln (I542/I440-1.03) versus L-Lys concentration.
The possible interaction mechanism of CDs with L-Lys.
(A) FL spectra of CDs at the pH value of 2 − 8. (B−D) Linear relationship between the I542/I440 versus pH.
LSCM images of HeLa cells after being incubated with CDs responds to L-Lys.
LSCM images of HeLa cells after being incubated with CDs at different pH values.
LSCM images of zebrafish treated with CDs and CDs/L-Lys.
LSCM images of zebrafish treated with CDs at pH 5 and 8.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent carbon dots for enantioselective sensing of L-lysine and pH discrimination in vivo and in vitro

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Institute of Molecular Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Institute of Molecular Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Institute of Molecular Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric fluorescent probes with wavelength-resolved dual target sensing capacity are highly desirable for the simultaneous recognition of target objects. A novel fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with a yield of 32% was simply synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of neutral red and urea, resulting in discrete dual-emission bands (440 nm/542 nm) under single-wavelength excitation of 380 nm. It was discovered that the fluorescence (FL) signal of CDs at 440 nm enhances with the addition of L-Lysine, whereas the FL intensity at 542 nm remains invariable. Surprisingly, no significant luminescence response is observed after the addition of D-Lysine, implying that as-prepared CDs can serve as fluorescent nanoprobes for chiral recognition of Lysine. On the contrary, it was discovered that the FL band at 542 nm is highly sensitive to pH in linear ranges of 2.0–3.4 and 3.4–8.0, while the FL band at 440 nm is inert to it, providing an effective tool for the ratiometric detection of pH in a “turn-on” mode. Confocal imaging experiments further verify that this nanoprobe can monitor L-Lysine and pH fluctuations in biological systems in real time using ratiometric methods. We believe that our research will provide new insight into the suitable construction of prompt and straightforward ratiometric fluorescent probes for the simultaneous differentiation of two analytes.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging type of carbon nanomaterial, have sparked increased attention as they incorporate numerous desirable features, including good optical properties, prominent chemical stability, and fabulous biocompatibility [1–3]. As a result, CDs have been recognized as a superior alternative to traditional fluorescent materials for bioimaging and biosensing applications [4–6]. As far as we know, fluorescent sensors for timely tracking and imaging are of great importance in medicine for quality control, biomedical analysis, and cancer treatment, as they provide powerful tools for monitoring appropriate signals and obtaining pivotal information in a short period of time. Multiple ratiometric fluorescent probes have been frequently designed and exhibit enormous potential in practical applications because they can improve the sensitivity and accuracy by avoiding interference from various target-independent factors and thus provide more reliable and precise data than single-wavelength intensity-modulated probes [7–9]. Several pieces of literature have been reported to date. For instance, Zhang et al. [10] constructed a reasonable ratiometric analysis terrace based on co-doped CDs for the purpose of detecting hypochlorite via oxidation reaction. Shangguan et al. [11] acquired dual photoluminescence emission CDs as a ratiometric fluorescent probe capable of sensing pH in aqueous solution and monitoring stimulus-responsive intracellular pH fluctuations in real time. Shuang’s group [12] proposed a facile strategy through hydrothermal treatment of alizarin carmine for designing a ratiometric sensing platform for specifically distinguishing cancer cells from normal cells by tracking the GSH expression level. Despite the recent development of a few fluorescent probes, single-analyte response still hinders the extensive applications of biological or biomedical analysis. For these reasons, the establishment of CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent probes with more functions for multitasking purposes remains a necessary and unfulfilled challenge.

Chirality is a well-known fundamental feature that has invoked increasing scientific interest because of its striking implications in actual applications involving pharmaceuticals, foods, and agricultural chemicals [13]. While chiral enantiomers have similar chemical structures, a pair of enantiomers has inconsistent, and in some cases, opposite effects, in a variety of physiological and biological activities [14]. To date, considerable efforts have been made for enantioselective identification. Although several chemical sensors exist, their sophisticated multistep synthesis and high equipment cost necessitated their development [15–17]. Thus, it is imperative to develop novel fluorescent sensors with a facile and inexpensive synthetic procedure for chiral recognition.

L-amino acids, as inseparable substances of all life, play vital roles in the maintenance of biological systems and are embroiled in a series of phenomena [18]. L-Lysine (L-Lys) is considered a semi-essential amino acid of considerable significance to human health and has a huge impact on enhancing immunity, promoting calcium absorption, and improving central nervous system function [19]. Assuredly, it participates in physiological activities including protein synthesis, energy metabolism, and polyamine synthesis. Nutritional anemia, growth retardation, and reduction of immunological function are all associated with low L-Lys levels, which is a risk factor for other diseases [20,21]. It is well known that the human body cannot synthesize L-Lys on its own and must rely on food supplements that contain it. With respect to the crucial efficacies of L-Lys, specific quantitation of L-Lys levels is indispensable. Surely, various kinds of sensors concerning L-Lys discrimination have been extensively researched. Dong et al. [22] synthesized a metal-organic framework (UiO-66-NH2) that can function as a suitable monitoring tool toward L-Lys with the FL “turn-on” mode via L-Lys adsorption into the pores and the hydrogen bonding interactions between UiO-66-NH2 and L-Lys. Zhang et al. [23] demonstrated a unique protocol for fluorescent detection of L-Lys using ovalbumin protected copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) that took advantage of the strong multi-site coordination between copper and L-Lys. Saurina et al. [24] reported amperometric sensors based on graphite-methacrylate rigid composites to detect L-Lys, which were composed of chemically immobilized L-Lys oxidase membranes attached to the surface of rigid conducting composites. Although these fluorescent probes have presented great potential in the quantification of L-Lys, most of them still encountered certain shortcomings, including intricate synthesis, tedious operation, and poor solubility, which made it difficult to extend their practical fields. More seriously, they also relied on single-wavelength intensity-modulated sensing. In these contexts, great efforts are very valuable to seek CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent probes that have easy preparation, superior specificity, and a fast response for visualizing L-Lys changes in living cells, particularly in vivo.

Using a hydrothermal method of neutral red and urea, dual-emission CDs with a yield of 32% was developed, which was then applied to design a ratiometric fluorescent strategy for precise measurement of L-Lys and pH. Unlike other fluorescent nanoprobes, the advantages of our CDs-based probes embody the following aspects: (1) When excited with a single wavelength, this probe has a unique wavelength-dependent dual-responsive capability to L-Lys and pH, allowing them to easily complete the simultaneous detection of two targets in aqueous solution in a ratiometric manner. (2) There is no need for complex preparation and cumbersome procedure, resulting in a simple, environmentally friendly, and fast strategy for distinguishing L-Lys from D-Lys. (3) Because of the distinctive behavior described above, as well as the minimal cytotoxicity, superior biocompatibility, and exceptional stability of CDs, real-time and label-free visual identification of L-Lys and pH variations can be achieved in vitro and in vivo. (4) Ascribed to the intrinsic dual-responsive feature, this probe is more beneficial for its applications not only in biosensing but also for real sample analysis.

Neutral red, urea, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2⋅2H2O, and MgSO4⋅7H2O were all purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was ordered from Sijiqing Bioengineering Materials (Hangzhou, China). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), alanine, arginine, aspartic acid, cysteine, histidine, isoleucine, leucine, glutamine, methionine, phenylalanine, glutathione, L-Lysine (L-Lys), D-Lysine (D-Lys), proline, threonine, tryptophan, and tyrosine were all purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). E3 culture medium includes NaCl, KCl, CaCl2⋅2 H2O, and MgSO4⋅7H2O.

Details of the preparation of CDs were shown as follows: 0.02 g of neutral red and 0.2 g of urea were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water, which was put through ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. Followed by the above steps, the mixture was allowed to undergo further reaction in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 4 h. Thereafter, these crude products were naturally cooled to ambient temperature. After centrifugation (3000 rpm, 30 min), the resultant solution was filtered with microporous membrane (0.22 µm) and further purified using dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500–1000 Da) for 18 h. Eventually, the purified solution of CDs was dried and then preserved in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of L-Lys and pH
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical run, 50 µL of CDs solution was diluted into 500 µL of PBS buffer. L-Lys with different amounts were separately added into the above CDs system and subjected to FL measurement (excited at 380 nm). As for the selectivity of CDs towards various amino acids, experiments were conducted instead of L-Lys in an identical way and their FL spectra were recorded.

The determination of pH was carried out at various pH in PBS buffer. At first, the PBS buffer was adjusted to the corresponding pH with acid (HCl) and base (NaOH) as modulators using the pH meter (pH 2.0, 2.2, 2.4, 2.6, 2.8, 3.0, 3.2, 3.4, 3.6, 3.8, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0). Then, 50 µL of CDs solution was diluted into 500 µL of PBS buffer under different pH values and their FL spectra were monitored.

HeLa cells were plated onto culture dish and grown overnight in DMEM equipped with 10% FBS in an atmosphere of air with 5% CO2. Then CDs (100 μg/mL) were added to the culture dish, and the cells were incubated at 37 °C. After 20 min, the mixture medium was removed, and the cells were washed three times with PBS buffer (pH=7). After that, images were captured by laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM). Next, the above cells were incubated with L-Lys (100 μM) for another 5 min in the culture dish. And images were captured by LSCM. For intracellular pH sensing, the remaining cells were randomly divided into three groups, and they were incubated for 10 min with CDs at different pH values of 4, 6, and 8, respectively. Then the cells were washed by PBS buffer three times. Again, images were captured by LSCM.

For bioimaging in vivo, the zebrafish (5 dpf) were fed with CDs (0.34 mg/mL) in E3 culture medium (5 mL) at 28 °C. After 4 h, the mixture medium was removed, and the zebrafish were washed with fresh E3 medium twice. Then images were captured by LSCM. For the positive control group, the CDs-stained zebrafish were incubated with L-Lys (600 μM) and washed with fresh E3 medium twice. Next, images were captured by LSCM. And in another study, the zebrafish were randomly divided into two groups, and they were incubated for 4 h with CDs at different pH values of 5 and 8, respectively. Then the zebrafish were washed with fresh E3 medium twice and then subjected to fluorescence imaging.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the chemical composition of CDs. The full scan spectrum (Fig. S1A) exhibits three representative peaks at 281.85, 397.56, and 529.71 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s. In detail, the C1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. S1B) is fitted into three components at 284.54 eV (CC), 285.49 eV (C−N), 287.21 eV (COOH). The spectrum of N1s in Fig. S1C is deconvoluted into two main peaks at 399.16 and 400.82 eV, which are in good agreement with pyridine N and pyrrole N [25]. The three peaks at 531.43, 532.58, and 533.45 eV arise from CO, C–OH, and C–O (Fig. S1D). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to investigate the functional groups of CDs. The broad absorption bands located between 3070 and 3462 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of O−H/N − H and the peak at 1474.37 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of C−H (Fig. S1E). Also, the specific peaks at 2847.67, 1591.48, 1397.49, and 1159.14 cm−1 show the presence of the stretching vibrations of C−H, CN, C−N, and C−O. Circular dichroism (CD) was used to reveal the chiroptical properties of CDs. As shown in Fig. S1F, no signal in the CD spectrum of CDs is observed, indicating that as-synthesized CDs are achiral. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) equipped with atomic force microscope (AFM) depict the morphology and size distribution of CDs. In Fig. S2, it is clear that the monodispersed particles can be observed in the scope with a mean particle diameter of 2.47 nm, suggesting the uniform dispersion and homogeneity of nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. S3, the CDs particles are spherical in shape with a typical topographic height of 4.82 nm, which matches with the size measured by TEM.

As shown in Fig. 1A, the absorption peak centers at 271.14 nm, which may derive from the π-π * transition in an aromatic conjugated system and was denoted as the core state accompanied by the highest energy level. However, another lower energy absorption band at 517.09 nm can be regarded as the surface state, corresponding to the n-π * transition of functional groups such as CO or C−N on the surface of CDs [26,27]. Fig. 1B indicates that CDs display intriguing dual-emission behavior under single-wavelength excitation. When excited at 380 nm, the CDs emit bright yellow FL (542 nm), but weak blue FL (440 nm). According to previous reports, it has been proven that blue emission peak of CDs probably arises from carbon core, while yellow emission peak is attributed to surface state of nitrogen-related speciation [28]. As revealed by XPS, different kinds of nitrogen speciation on the surface of CDs form N related surface states, which can build up a series of nitrogen-related energy states and narrow the energy levels, eventually leading to a yellow FL emission. A quantum yield of CDs is 12.8% using Rhodamine 6 G as a standard. For biological applications, appraising the stability of fluorescent CDs is of great need. More detailed investigation demonstrates that the FL intensity is almost invariable even in a high sodium ion environment (Fig. S4A), confirming that as-prepared CDs are more beneficial for its applications in the biochemical environment of high-salinity sample matrix. As expected, virtually no influence on the FL intensity of CDs is seen during long-term storage (Fig. S4B). In short, superior stability of CDs is conducive to extend its application for analysis of complex samples.

Optical responses of CDs to L-Lys (as a model) were carried out. As we designed, only L-Lys induces obvious FL enhancement at 440 nm, while other amino acids show negligible changes, indicating that the probe can detect L-Lys with relatively high selectivity (Fig. S5). Results indicate that no significant change is seen for D-Lys, which may be due to the differential binding affinity of CDs and chiral species, providing a simple enantio-sensing strategy for chiral recognition of L/D-Lys. With the increase of L-Lys, the FL intensity of the system gradually increases at 440 nm, while that at 542 nm remains unchanged ( Fig. 2 A). A good linear relationship is presented by plotting the Ln (I542/I440-1.03) and L-Lys concentration in the range of 0.1 μM− 700 μM, where the regression equation is Y= −0.00474x + 1.050 with R2 = 0.998 (Fig. 2B). The detection limit is 19 nM, which is comparable or much lower than those of reported probes for L-Lys determination.

The high selectivity of CDs towards L-Lys can be ascribed to the hydrogen bond interactions between NH3 + and the oxygen-containing functional groups (–COOH, and –OH) [21]. Among the tested amino acids, all exist in the zwitterionic form owing to the adjacent basic amino and acidic carboxyl groups [29]. However, L-Lys is the only amino acid with a free amino group far from the zwitterionic center, and it is a cationic residue with a long (CH2)4NH3 + side chain [30]. As-prepared CDs have a large surface to volume ratio with abundant functional groups, including –COOH and –OH. Compared with other amino acids, the CDs surface moieties preferentially bind with L-Lys NH3 + through hydrogen bond interactions ( Fig. 3). To further verify this hypothesis, zeta potential of CDs before and after adding L-Lys were carried out. As can be seen in Fig. S6, the zeta potential of CDs aqueous solution is − 13.6 mV, indicating the partial ionization of –COOH/–OH attached to the surface of CDs. However, result confirms that the zeta potential increases inversely to be − 3.8 mV by exposure of L-Lys, showing the consumption of –COOH/–OH and the successful combination of L-Lys with the negative charges of oxygen-containing groups [31]. In addition, UV–vis absorption of CDs and CDs/L-Lys were recorded in Fig. S7. Upon addition of L-Lys to the CDs system, an obvious shift of the π-π * transition peak appears, and it can be inferred that the interaction between CDs and L-Lys occurs. The calculated average lifetimes of CDs and CDs/L-Lys are 4.02 ns and 4.35 ns, respectively (Fig. S8). The difference between the two values is very small, which confirms that the FL response mechanism of CDs to L-Lys may be related to a static process. As shown in Fig. S9, after addition of L-Lys, no evident change is observed, which implies that the quasi-spherical CDs do not aggregate together with other particles. Based on these above-mentioned evidences, the interaction principle for the probe to L-Lys supports the static mechanism that –COOH/–OH on the surface of CDs reacts with L-Lys to form CDs/L-Lys conjugate through hydrogen bonds, enhancing the π-conjugation and restricting the rotational vibrations of functional groups on the CDs surface, thereby increasing the FL of CDs [32].

Understanding the chiral recognition process at molecular level is of vital importance to clarify experimental results. Molecular docking is a convenient and fast method, and it is also one of the most effective tools to explore the enantioselective sensing mechanism because the interactions and structural properties between enantiomers and sensors can be visualized and compared [33–37]. However, the mechanism of chiral recognition can be studied most effectively when the exact structure of sensors is known. Thus, an achiral carbon dots (ACDs) was selected as a model structure to further study chiral recognition mechanism [38].

Molecular docking studies were carried out using AutoDock Vina by Genetic Algorithm. As depicted in Fig. S10, the binding modes of two enantiomers with ACDs show significant differences. Hydrogen bond interaction plays a leading role in the formation of complexes. The models indicate the differential conformations of two enantiomers to ACDs. Table S1 enlists the binding free energies of complexes. It is discovered that there is an obvious difference in binding free energy [13]. Based on the docking results, we speculate that the difference in binding free energy between as-synthesized CDs and two enantiomers contributes to the difference in fluorescence response and establishes the chiral recognition results [39–41].

As shown in Fig. 4A, by the increase of pH in the range of 2 − 8, the FL intensity of CDs at 542 nm enhances significantly while the FL intensity shows slight variations at 440 nm, which suggests that the FL intensity of CDs at 542 nm is sensitive to pH, while that at 440 nm is inert to it. Note in Fig. 4B–D that there are two good linear relationships between the FL intensity ratio (I542/I440) and pH in the ranges of 2–3.4 (R2 =0.999) and 3.4–8 (R2 =0.996), confirming that the CDs can be used as a sensitive probe for ratiometric detection of pH.

It is worth mentioning that when the pH value varies from 2 to 3.4, the ratio of FL intensity (I542/I440) increases rapidly, and when the pH value is above 3.4, the ratio of FL intensity shows a slight increase. This phenomenon may be attributed to the special structure of CDs [42–45]. XPS analysis demonstrates that the nitrogen primarily exists in the form of pyridine and pyrrole. As we all know, the alkalinity of pyridinic is relatively stronger than pyrrolic because the lone pair of electrons on the pyrrolic nitrogen participates in p-π conjugation [46,47]. In the experiments, the nitrogen atoms on pyridine ring are firstly protonated. As the pH value gradually decreases, the nitrogen atoms on pyrrole are subsequently protonated. The unique structure of CDs make it show two linear relationships in the pH range of 2.0–3.4 and 3.4–8. To prove this speculation of pH sensing, we have also examined the hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential at different pH values. Fig. S11 demonstrates that the hydrated particle sizes of CDs have no obvious changes in both acidic and alkaline media by comparison with that at pH 7, implying that CDs are dissolved as isolated species without the appearance of aggregation. It is clear that the zeta potential of CDs aqueous solution become more negative as the increase of pH (Fig. S12), revealing the protonation and protonation of functional groups [48–50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent imaging in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Monitoring and analyzing three-dimensional structure of living cells in real time with high temporal and spatial resolution is extremely significant for the recognition of intracellular targets and the research of disease diagnosis. Accordingly, we assessed the performance of the probe for L-Lys/pH sensing in HeLa cells by LSCM under irradiation of 405 nm laser. It is well known that cytotoxicity of the probe is a cardinal factor for biological applications. Fig. S13 signifies that at a very high 100 μg/mL, the cells keep high viability of above 77%, proving the low cytotoxicity of CDs. Fig. 5 displays that despite the blue FL fluctuating after the addition of L-Lys, no apparent variation of the yellow FL can be visualized, showing that as-developed probe is useful for ratiometric imaging of L-Lys in living cells.

Intracellular pH is a paramount regulative factor of biochemical reactions in cell biology and highly regulated in every organelle such as lysosomes and mitochondria, which plays a pivotal role in physiological and pathological processes, such as DNA replication, enzymatic activity, and muscle contraction [51–53]. Virtually all proteins rely on pH to modulate their structure and function, and intracellular pH micro-fluctuation may cause physiological dysfunction or adverse illnesses. Owing to its vital significance, the pH of cells is defended at multiple levels and stringently regulated for maintaining normal life movements. Thus, tracking intracellular pH accurately is very beneficial for understanding cellular functions and giving a basis for early diagnosis of cancer. In further experiments, as we designed, real-time FL imaging of pH dynamics in living cells by CDs was performed. With the increase of pH values, the yellow FL emission exhibits distinct enhancement, while the blue FL intensity has almost no variation ( Fig. 6). Results signify that the probe is a powerful tool to monitor pH and L-Lys in real-time at the cellular levels.

Prompted by the exciting observations from in vitro experiments, we also evaluated the applicability of the probe in living animals. There is no doubt that we initially investigated long-term survivability of zebrafish against different CDs concentrations. After treatment with probe even at the dosage of 0.5 mg/mL, zebrafish have no marked reduction in numbers (Fig. S14), confirming that the probe have no negative impacts on zebrafish growth and can be applied as potential biolabeling agent in living animals. As depicted in Fig. 7, weak FL exists in blue channel and bright FL appears in yellow channel. By contrast, it is evident that blue emission increases while yellow one almost keeps constant after adding L-Lys into the culture dish. Next, we studied the ability of the probe to monitor pH in zebrafish. As expected, with the raise of the pH values, a stronger yellow FL signal can be seen in zebrafish after treatment with CDs, but a blue FL signal is nearly steady ( Fig. 8). The identical FL phenomenon in the animals expresses the as-prepared CDs can function as an ideal nanoprobe for the real-time detection of pH variations in living animals. The FL signals concentrate in the digestive system and spread over the zebrafish body such as tissues, organs, and even skeletal structures, which indicates that the probe can be rapidly adsorbed into the zebrafish through the chorion and mouth. Further, as far as we know, this is the first time that ratiometric fluorescent CDs have been used for the simultaneous differentiation of two analytes at the cellular and in vivo levels.

In conclusion, dual-emission CDs were synthesized via direct carbonization treatment of neutral red and urea. As we designed, this probe displays a unique dual-responsive functionality to Lys enantiomers and pH under single-wavelength excitation, enabling a desirable and rapid ratiometric detection strategy for these two targets. Impressively, it was discovered that benefiting from excellent features of low cytotoxicity, remarkable biocompatibility, and distinctive FL behavior, this probe can be applied as a visual analytical platform for ratiometrically tracking stimulus-responsive fluctuations of pH and L-Lys both in living cells and in zebrafish with acceptable results. It is anticipated that our developed probe has great practical application prospects for the diagnosis of L-Lys and pH-related diseases and disorders.

Dan Chang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zhonghua Zhao: Investigation, Validation. Hu Shi: Investigation, Validation. Jianyang Feng: Investigation, Validation. Yongxing Yang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Lihong Shi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131792.

Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of red emissive carbon dots: robust performance for analytical applications in pesticide monitoring
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.X. Li, D.D. Su, H. Gao, X. Yan, D.S. Kong, R. Jin, X.M. Liu, C.G. Wang, G.Y. Lu, Design of red emissive carbon dots: robust performance for analytical applications in pesticide monitoring, Anal. Chem. 92 (2020) 3198–3205.

Anal. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of acidic pH in lysosome with carbon nanodots
Chin. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Y. He, Z.X. Li, Q.Y. Jia, B.J. Shi, H.Y. Zhang, L.H. Wei, M.M. Yu, Ratiometric fluorescent detection of acidic pH in lysosome with carbon nanodots, Chin. Chem. Lett. 28 (2017) 1969–1974.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dan Chang obtained B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Xinzhou Teachers University in 2017. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in physical chemistry.

Zhonghua Zhao is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi province, China. He obtained his Ph.D. degree in 2010. His current research interests include human disease genetics and animal model.

Hu Shi is a professor in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering in Shanxi University, China. His main research interests include the understanding of protein aggregation mechanisms, biosensor properties, chemical reactions, and 2D material properties.
Jianyang Feng is now a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in materials and chemical engineering.

Yongxing Yang is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. He obtained his Ph.D. degree from Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. His current research interests include material chemistry and industrial catalysis.

Lihong Shi is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.
